lines.text: ...who's that?
lines.action: whispers to friend, not bothering to lower her voice much

lines.text: New. Obviously.
lines.action: doesn't even look up from his phone

lines.text: Room 214. Ten seconds. Maybe less.
lines.action: the door looks heavier than a door should

lines.text: There they are. Come on in, don't let the door hit you on the way — grab a seat, any seat. We don't bite. Usually.
lines.action: waves you in without looking up from his roster

lines.text: Hi. I mean — hey. There's, um, there's a seat here, if you—
lines.action: sits up too fast, pencil rolling off the desk

lines.text: Oh my gosh, hi! Come sit with us, there's totally room — right, Deja?
lines.action: already patting the empty chair before you've answered

lines.text: There's room if Priya moves, like, one (1) item off the table.
lines.action: taps a quick rhythm on the desk with two fingers

lines.text: I have three things. Three. That's not a crime.
lines.action: laughing, doesn't move the three things

lines.text: In the back corner, a kid with headphones half-on keeps their eyes on their notebook. Nobody waves them over. Nobody looks at them at all.
lines.action: Alex doesn't look up

lines.text: So — did you know our mascot's a fighting mountain goat? Ridgeline Rams. Except goats don't ram, that's — that's rams, so it, uh. It checks out, I guess.
lines.action: immediately looks like he wants to sink into the floor

lines.text: ...I'll stop talking now.
lines.action: goes back to doodling like the desk is very interesting

lines.text: Okay, obsessed with your bag, where is that from?
lines.action: leaning in like you're already old friends

lines.text: Wait, say the school you came from again? I'm putting it together in my head.
lines.action: already typing something on her phone

lines.text: Okay, not to be that person, but — has Alex said one word since sixth grade? Like, blink twice if you're in there.
lines.action: nods toward the back corner without lowering her voice, watching your face

lines.text: Not to be dramatic, but yeah. Kind of a whole vibe.
lines.action: shrugs like it's just a fact of nature

lines.text: Maya—
lines.action: taps the desk once, doesn't finish the sentence, laughs instead like she didn't mean to say anything

lines.text: Alright, partners for the semester project — no trading, no negotiating, I already know your tricks. Alex Rivera and... you.
lines.action: doesn't look up from the list

lines.text: You can ask to switch. I won't be weird about it.
lines.action: already gathering their stuff like they expect to be moved

lines.text: Alex sits alone, still sorting index cards into neat, useless piles. Across the room, someone's waving you over.
lines.action: tray in hand, decision time

lines.text: Over here! We saved you a spot!
lines.action: patting the bench, already scooting over

lines.text: Oops.
lines.action: bumps the table just hard enough — Alex's papers slide to the floor

lines.text: Guess gravity hates group projects too.
lines.action: doesn't move to help

lines.text: Okay that was kind of an accident—
lines.action: laughs anyway, glancing at Maya to check if that was funny
laughs a beat too late, then doesn't laugh at all — taps her knuckles against her tray, eyes on Alex

lines.text: It's fine.
lines.action: already crouching to gather the papers, not looking at anyone

lines.text: ...Should we—
lines.action: half-stands, looks at you instead of finishing the sentence

lines.text: The cafeteria's gone quiet in that specific way, where everyone's watching without watching. Waiting to see where you land.
The first bell of the year has come and gone. Somewhere between the hallway and the cafeteria, people decided who you are.
lines.action: waiting at the bus stop

options.text: Shoulders back. Easy nod. Own it.
Duck in quiet. Read the room first.
Big smile, wave at everyone — worth a shot.

b.text: Nobody notices you slide in. That's kind of the point. That's also kind of the problem.

c.text: (a beat of confused silence before someone half-waves back)

options.text: Sit with Jordan — he actually seems nice.
Sit with Maya's table — looks like the place to be.

a.text: Oh — cool. Cool cool cool. You can, um, you can have the chair, I don't need the chair.

options.text: Laugh for real — okay, that was actually funny.
Smile politely, ask him something else.
Look away, unsure what to say.

a.text: Wait, really? Okay — okay that's the first time that's landed all year.
I draw, um. Comics, kind of. Bad ones. Well — I think they're okay.

b.text: Yeah — no, yeah, it wasn't that funny. So. What's your first period?

c.action: goes back to doodling, doesn't look up again for a while

options.text: Laugh along — easiest way to fit in.
"Anyway — what class do you guys have next?"
"I don't know, Alex seems fine to me."

a.text: Right? I knew you'd get it.
(laughs, but it doesn't quite reach her eyes)

b.text: Ooh, smooth. Fine, be mysterious. AP Bio, unfortunately.
(exhales, quiet, like she'd been holding that one)

c.text: (glances at you, something like surprise, doesn't say anything — but she's tapping the desk again, faster now)

options.text: "No, I'm actually glad we got paired."
"Cool. So what's the project even on?"
(glance toward your friend group first)

a.text: Okay. Cool. I mean — cool, that's — okay.
a.emotion: doesn't know what to do with sincerity

b.text: Local history thing. There's a packet. It's not exciting, but it's not hard either.

c.action: goes back to their notebook before you even answer

options.text: Sit with Alex, keep working on the project.
Sit with your group — you've got a spot there now.
"Hey — come sit with us?"

b.text: There you are! Okay, sit, sit, you have to hear what Priya's mom said this morning.

c.emotion: small crack, unsure what to do with it

options.text: "Maya. Seriously. Not okay."
(kneel down, help gather the papers, say nothing)
(stay back — not really your place)
(laugh along, keep the peace)

a.emotion: genuinely caught off guard, mask slipping

a.action: starts helping pick up papers too, not looking at Maya

c.text: (gathers the papers alone, doesn't look at anyone, jaw tight)
(shifts, looks at his shoes, doesn't move either)

d.text: See? Even the new kid gets it.
(doesn't react at all — face just closes, like a door)
(smiles, but it's the kind of smile that doesn't reach anywhere)

voiceNotes:
CASTING NOTE: The provided character profile is for Jules Okafor-Rivas, but Jules doesn't appear by name in any scene's character list. Rather than leaving that voice unused, I folded Jules's specific verbal DNA — fast-talking deflection, trailing off right when something matters, the desk-tapping (drummer's tell), the code-switch between blunt and careful — into 'Deja,' Maya's friend who was otherwise just a name with no interiority. This gives the crew a fracture point: Deja laughs along in scene-3b/6 but never fully commits to the cruelty, which sets up believable friction if the player earns her trust. If Jules is meant to appear as a separate, distinct character elsewhere in the episode, her lines can be lifted wholesale from Deja's — the voice work was built for exactly that profile.

MR. OKAFOR: Brisk, dry, dad-energy — the kind of teacher who's clearly said 'we don't bite, usually' every first day for a decade. Doesn't hover, doesn't perform kindness, just quietly makes room for you without making it a thing.

JORDAN: Runs sentences into the ground when nervous, self-interrupts, over-explains jokes that didn't need explaining. Vulnerability is loud and visible on him — he doesn't have Jules's gift for deflection, so every awkward beat lands exposed. This makes him easy to either wound or befriend cheaply, which is the point of his low-stakes test.

MAYA CHEN: Charm as currency. Every compliment is also a transaction — she's testing what you'll trade for a seat at her table. Her dig at Alex is delivered light, almost affectionate, specifically so it's deniable ('I was just joking') — this is what makes it insidious rather than obviously villainous.

PRIYA: Underwritten by design — she's a mirror for Maya, laughing a half-beat after to check the room first. Not cruel on her own, just uncommitted to anything that costs her anything.

ALEX RIVERA: The opposite of Jules — where Jules deflects with jokes, Alex deflects with pre-emptive surrender ('you can ask to switch, I won't be weird about it'). Short sentences, flat affect, clearly rehearsed lines that come from having done this exact scene with other partners before. The cracks (the 'oh,' the unfinished 'you didn't have to—') are where the real feeling leaks through, and they should feel expensive, not easy.

GENERAL: Nobody in this episode says 'I feel excluded' or 'that hurt my feelings.' The hurt shows up sideways — in what Alex does with their hands, in Deja's laugh that's a half-second late, in Jordan going quiet instead of finishing a sentence. Resist any urge to have characters narrate their own emotional state directly; let the player infer it from behavior.

alternatives: You don't have to do the whole 'it's fine' thing. I know how this goes.
If you want to switch, just tell Mr. O. I get it.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Original keeps Alex's guard fully up and pre-emptive; these alternates let a touch more visible hurt through, which could work better if you want the audience to sympathize with Alex faster, at the cost of some of the deadpan restraint that makes the later cracks land harder.

alternatives: Not to start drama, but does Alex, like, talk? At all? Ever?
Okay, weird question — is Alex okay? Like actually okay?
alternativeLines.reasoning: The second alt is intentionally more ambiguous — reads as concern OR as a setup for mockery, forcing the player to actually read tone rather than react to obvious cruelty. Riskier but could deepen the 'test' framing if Maya is meant to be more slippery than outright mean.

alternatives: (bumps the table 'accidentally,' watching to see who laughs)
My bad. Guess we bumped it.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Original 'Oops' is clean and cutting in its brevity — matches Maya's performative deniability. The alternates spell out the intent more, which trades subtlety for clarity if playtesters find the original too subtle to register as a real transgression.

alternatives: (doesn't laugh at all this time, just watches Alex's hands shake picking up the papers)
alternativeLines.reasoning: The second alt has Deja speak up unprompted before the player's choice, which could undercut the player's own agency in choice-6 — kept the original silent version so the moral weight of intervening stays with the player, not an NPC.
